title: The Great Depression
summary: The most severe global economic crisis of the 20th century — triggered by the Wall Street Crash of 1929, propagated by bank failures, trade collapse, and deflationary spirals, and ending only with World War II mobilization — which permanently changed how governments understood their responsibility for economic stability and generated both the New Deal and the political conditions for fascism.
hook: At its worst, one in four Americans was unemployed. Banks closed by the thousands. Farm prices collapsed. The conventional wisdom said governments should do nothing — that the economy would correct itself. The Depression didn't just destroy livelihoods. It destroyed a theory of government. What came after — the welfare state, Keynesian economics, deposit insurance, financial regulation — was built on the ruins.

plainEnglish.theEvent:
By 1933, one in four American workers could not find work. Men who had built cars or sold insurance now stood in line for a bowl of free soup. Savings built up over years were gone. The banks holding that money had shut their doors for good.

The trouble started in October 1929. Stock prices on the New York Stock Exchange fell very fast. Stocks are shares in companies that people buy and sell. When prices fell, businesses cut production and sent workers home. Those workers had no money to buy things, so more businesses failed. More people lost their jobs. This cycle fed on itself for four years.

Industrial production fell by half. The prosperous 1920s became the desperate 1930s very fast. This was not only an American crisis. It spread around the world and lasted a decade.
plainEnglish.context:
The Depression did not appear from nowhere. The 1920s had been a decade of wild speculation. People were buying and selling stocks hoping to make fast money. Stock prices had climbed far above what companies actually earned. Many investors borrowed money to buy more stocks. They were betting that prices would keep rising. Banks were lightly regulated and easy to break.

The wider world was fragile too. Most large economies used the gold standard. This system tied each currency to gold at a fixed rate. That kept money stable in good times. But it tied governments' hands in a crisis.

When the crash hit, each weakness made the others worse. The fall in stock prices pushed banks under. Failing banks stopped lending money. The gold standard blocked relief. New tariffs, which are taxes on goods from other countries, sparked revenge tariffs abroad. Trade fell apart.
plainEnglish.theRecord:
In the United States, unemployment reached roughly 25 percent in 1933. In Germany it climbed nearly as high. This fed a political crisis that helped carry Hitler to power.

Nature added its own blow. Across the plains, drought and worn-out farming stripped the soil from millions of acres. Dust storms blotted out the sun. This disaster was called the Dust Bowl. Hundreds of thousands of families drove west toward California, which often did not want them.

In the cities, men waited hours for soup. Shacks built from scrap wood filled public parks. The Depression marked everyone who lived through it. Many stayed wary of good times for life.
plainEnglish.phases:
The Depression came in waves. The 1929 crash wiped out paper wealth. But the banking crisis of 1930 to 1933 did far deeper damage. When a bank failed, depositors lost their savings. Businesses lost the credit they ran on. The slump sped up.

The global side made everything heavier. American tariffs triggered foreign revenge. World trade shrank. Countries clinging to the gold standard could not print more money without breaking their promise to back it with gold.

Then came a turn. Roosevelt's election and his New Deal steadied the economy and built lasting protections. But in 1937, worried about the budget, he cut spending too soon. The economy tumbled back into recession. Real recovery arrived only when World War II spending put people back to work.
plainEnglish.mechanics:
The Depression's depth was not fate. Choices that looked sensible at the time made it worse.

As thousands of banks collapsed, the Federal Reserve stayed passive. The Federal Reserve is the country's central bank. It let the money supply shrink by about a third. This turned a hard recession into a catastrophe.

Governments tied to the gold standard kept interest rates high. High interest rates were meant to stop gold from leaving the country. But high rates were the wrong medicine for an economy that was already shrinking.

And when leaders did act, many raised taxes and cut spending to balance their books. That was the accepted wisdom at the time. Stacked together, these moves dragged the economy down far further than it needed to fall.
plainEnglish.transformation:
The Depression changed what people expected their government to do.

Before 1929, the common belief was that markets fixed themselves. A downturn was just part of a natural cycle. Government meddling would only make it worse.

The Depression shattered that faith. A new idea took hold. When private spending collapses, the government should step in and spend to fill the gap. The British thinker John Maynard Keynes gave this approach its clearest argument. The New Deal put pieces of it into practice. It created Social Security, insurance for bank deposits, rules for the stock market, and legal protection for unions.
plainEnglish.causation:
People have argued about what caused the Depression for nearly a century.

One explanation blames the Federal Reserve. It let banks fail and let the money supply vanish. A second blames the collapse of spending, with too little government action to replace it. A third blames the gold standard for carrying the American crisis around the world.

Most experts now accept all three at once. The Depression was several failures feeding off each other rather than one cause. What people still debate is the weight of each.
plainEnglish.significance:
The Depression's political effects were as large as its economic ones.

In the United States it built a voting bloc that shaped politics for a generation. In Germany the results were far darker. Mass joblessness and despair drained the young democracy of trust. By 1932, about a quarter of German workers had no job. That mix of shame, despair, and a shaky government opened the door for the Nazis. Economic pain alone did not create Nazism, but it cleared space for it to grow.

After the war, survivors of the Depression built new bodies to guard against another. The International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, and shared trade rules were all built by people set on preventing a repeat.

beginner.theEvent:
By 1933, one in four American workers could not find a job. Men who had built cars or sold insurance now stood in line for a bowl of free soup. Savings set aside over years were gone, locked inside banks that had shut their doors for good.

It had started in October 1929, when stock prices on the New York Stock Exchange collapsed. Businesses cut production and laid off workers. Those workers could no longer buy goods, so more businesses failed and more lost their jobs. The spiral fed on itself for four years.

Industrial production fell by half, and the prosperous 1920s became the desperate 1930s with shocking speed. This was not only an American crisis. It spread around the world and lasted a decade.
beginner.context:
The Depression did not come from nowhere. The 1920s had been a decade of wild speculation. Stock prices had climbed far above what companies actually earned. Many investors borrowed money to buy more stocks, betting prices would keep rising, and banks were lightly regulated and easy to break.

The wider world was fragile too. Most large economies used the gold standard, which tied each currency to gold at a fixed rate. That kept money stable in good times, but it tied governments' hands in a crisis.

When the crash hit, each weakness made the others worse. Stock collapse pushed banks under, failing banks choked off lending, and the gold standard blocked relief. New tariffs sparked revenge tariffs abroad, and trade fell apart.

In the United States, unemployment reached roughly 25 percent in 1933. In Germany it climbed nearly as high, feeding a political crisis that helped carry Hitler to power.

Nature added its own blow. Across the plains, drought and worn-out farming stripped the soil from millions of acres. Dust storms blotted out the sun, a disaster called the Dust Bowl. Hundreds of thousands of families drove west toward California, which often did not want them.

In the cities, men waited hours for soup, and shacks built from scrap wood filled public parks. The Depression marked everyone who lived through it, and many stayed wary of good times for life.

Then came a turn. Roosevelt's election and his New Deal steadied the economy and built lasting protections. But in 1937, worried about the budget, he cut spending too soon and the economy tumbled back into recession. Real recovery arrived only when World War II spending put people back to work.

And when leaders did act, many raised taxes and cut spending to balance their books, since that was the accepted wisdom. Stacked together, these moves dragged the economy down far further than it needed to fall.

Before 1929, the common belief was that markets fixed themselves. A downturn was just part of a natural cycle, and government meddling would only make it worse.

The Depression shattered that faith. A new idea took hold: when private spending collapses, the government should step in and spend to fill the gap. The British thinker John Maynard Keynes gave this approach its clearest argument. The New Deal put pieces of it into practice, creating Social Security, insurance for bank deposits, rules for the stock market, and legal protection for unions.

One explanation blames the Federal Reserve for letting banks fail and money vanish. A second blames the collapse of spending, with too little government action to replace it. A third blames the gold standard for carrying the American crisis around the world.

Most experts now accept all three at once. The Depression was several failures feeding off each other rather than one cause. What people still debate is the weight of each.

The Depression's political effects were as large as its economic ones.

In the United States it built a voting coalition that shaped politics for a generation. In Germany the results were far darker. Mass unemployment and despair drained the young democracy of trust. By 1932, about a quarter of German workers had no job. That mix of humiliation, desperation, and a shaky government opened the door for the Nazis. Economic pain alone did not create Nazism, but it cleared space for it to grow.

After the war, survivors of the Depression built new institutions to guard against another. The International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, and shared trade rules were all designed by people determined to prevent a repeat.

general.theEvent:
In 1933, roughly a quarter of American workers had no job, thousands of banks sat shuttered, and shantytowns spread across public parks. The crisis had begun with the October 1929 stock collapse, but the crash alone did not have to produce a decade of misery. What turned a sharp downturn into the defining catastrophe of the century was a sequence of policy choices that looked prudent at the time.

The Federal Reserve stood aside as banks failed. Governments balanced budgets in the middle of a contraction. The gold standard locked countries into rigid money policies. Each reflected the conventional economic wisdom of the period, and seeing why that wisdom was wrong took economists a generation. Several of the leading accounts, by figures such as Milton Friedman and the economic historian Barry Eichengreen, were written decades after the events they explain.
general.context:
The gold standard is the key to understanding why the slump spread worldwide. Under it, each currency was fixed to a set amount of gold, which kept exchange rates stable but stopped governments from expanding the money supply when they most needed to. Countries that clung to gold tended to suffer longer and deeper depressions than those that let go.

The pattern is hard to ignore. Britain left gold in 1931 and recovery followed. The United States left in 1933 and its recovery followed. France held on until 1936, and its rebound came last of all.

The international economist Charles Kindleberger added a second insight. He argued that the slump grew so severe partly because no single power was willing and able to steady the global system. Britain could no longer play that role, and the United States was capable but not yet willing. Each country chased its own interest while the shared system unraveled.
general.theRecord:
The human texture of the Depression survives most vividly in the testimony of people who lived it. The writer Studs Terkel collected oral histories that capture the experience directly, while John Steinbeck's novel of Dust Bowl migrants turned the statistics into faces and names.

Those accounts expose the gap between a number and a life. A 25 percent unemployment rate means three out of four workers still had jobs, yet it also means that almost every family either faced unemployment or watched neighbors face it. The fear rippled well beyond the officially jobless.

This is why the Depression registered as more than a chart of falling output. It became a shared experience of insecurity and humiliation that reshaped how a generation thought about economic life and about what a government owes its citizens. That changed expectation outlasted the crisis itself.
general.phases:
Roosevelt's 1937 reversal is the closest thing the Depression offers to a controlled experiment. Under pressure to shrink the deficit, he cut spending and allowed taxes to rise, and the economy promptly slid into a sharp recession. Industrial production dropped and unemployment climbed back toward earlier levels.

The episode supplied direct evidence that recovery had leaned on government spending more than many had admitted. Cutting that support too early proved costly, which is roughly what the followers of Keynes had warned.

The 1937 setback also reveals the politics tangled up in the economics. Pressure to balance the budget came from bond markets, business opinion, and conservatives who had resisted New Deal spending from the start. Sustaining public willingness to run deficits across years of hardship turned out to be as hard as getting the economics right, and historians still weigh how avoidable the reversal was.
general.mechanics:
One influential line of analysis, developed by the economist Ben Bernanke, argued that the banking collapse hurt the economy through a channel beyond the shrinking money supply that earlier accounts had stressed. When banks failed, they destroyed the relationships and local knowledge that let lenders judge who could be trusted with credit. Rebuilding those networks took years, even after money conditions stabilized, which helps explain why recovery dragged on.

That research did not stay in the archives. As chairman of the Federal Reserve during the 2008 crisis, Bernanke acted on it, fighting to prevent bank failures and keep credit flowing even when the cost drew fierce criticism. His reading of the Depression directly shaped how the next great crisis was managed. The episode is a reminder that historical interpretation can carry very practical stakes.
general.transformation:
The New Deal transformed American capitalism, but its design carried a deep compromise that is essential to understanding it. The coalition behind it depended on Southern Democrats, who set a firm condition: the new programs offering economic security had to be built so that they excluded or shortchanged Black Americans.

The historian Ira Katznelson has traced how this worked in practice. Agricultural and domestic workers, jobs held disproportionately by Black Americans, were left out of Social Security and key labor protections. Housing programs entrenched segregation rather than easing it.

This was not a footnote to the New Deal. It was woven into its core bargain. The same institutions that gave millions of workers a new floor of security also hardened racial inequality, and that legacy shaped American life for generations after the Depression itself had passed.
general.causation:
The various explanations for the Depression have converged on a practical consensus: the disaster came from several failures reinforcing one another, so guarding against another requires addressing all of them. Yet the agreement hides a lasting argument about emphasis.

Some economists stress that the Federal Reserve could have softened the blow by acting as a lender of last resort. Others counter that money policy alone cannot stop a severe collapse in demand, and that government spending is also necessary. A third group points to the gold standard, which made national remedies nearly impossible while it lasted.

Those differing emphases lead to different prescriptions. They help explain why the responses to the 2008 and 2020 crises combined heavy central bank intervention, large government spending, and international coordination, each drawing on a separate strand of what the Depression seemed to teach.
general.significance:
The Depression's part in the rise of Nazism was real but not simple. Economic desperation created the political opening for the Nazi movement, yet it did not dictate what that movement would do with power. The historian Adam Tooze has shown how the specific shape of German recovery under the Nazis, built on rearmament and self-sufficiency, set a path toward war that the Depression's origins alone did not determine.

The Depression's most lasting legacy may be institutional rather than directly political. The architects of the postwar order — who created the IMF, the World Bank, and a framework for governing international trade — had watched the slump tear apart global cooperation in the 1930s. Building those institutions was their attempt to make sure it could not happen again. Whether they succeeded in preventing another Depression, or simply delayed one, is a question economists still debate.

advanced.theEvent: The historical debate about the Depression has been shaped by the availability of new data and by the experience of subsequent crises. Christina Romer's retrospective construction of pre-Depression unemployment statistics substantially revised understanding of the Depression's magnitude relative to other downturns. The 2008 financial crisis generated a new wave of Depression scholarship — economists and historians sought to understand how the Depression had been handled and what lessons it offered for the contemporary crisis.
advanced.context: The relationship between the Depression and the political economy of the interwar period — the instability of the gold standard, the war debts and reparations problem, the political fragility of the Weimar Republic — has been analyzed by scholars including Harold James (The End of Globalization, 2001). James argues that the interwar period represented a globalization that failed — that the international economic integration of the 1920s proved fragile and that its collapse contributed to the political catastrophes of the 1930s. This analysis has been applied to contemporary debates about globalization's political vulnerabilities.
advanced.phases: The comparative analysis of Depression recovery — why some countries recovered faster than others — has been one of the most productive research programs in economic history. Eichengreen and Jeffrey Sachs's finding that countries that left the gold standard earlier recovered faster is one of the most robust results in the literature. The UK's departure from gold in 1931 (earlier than the US), France's later departure (1936), and the relationship between monetary freedom and recovery timing provides natural experimental evidence for the gold standard's role in the Depression's depth and duration.
advanced.mechanics: The debate about the Depression's relationship to the 2008 financial crisis has generated substantial new scholarship. The parallels — financial sector collapse, credit contraction, policy response — and differences — the 2008 response was much more aggressive, preventing a Depression-scale contraction — have been analyzed by scholars including Gary Gorton (Slapped by the Invisible Hand, 2010) and Atif Mian and Amir Sufi (House of Debt, 2014). Bernanke's memoir (The Courage to Act, 2015) provides the insider account of how Depression lessons shaped the 2008 policy response.
advanced.transformation: The political economy of the New Deal — who it helped, who it excluded, and what institutional legacy it left — has been substantially analyzed by historians including Katznelson, whose Fear Itself argues that the New Deal was fundamentally shaped by the Southern Democratic bloc's insistence on preserving racial apartheid. Agricultural workers and domestic servants — disproportionately Black — were excluded from Social Security and the National Labor Relations Act. The New Deal's racial compromises shaped American social policy for decades.
advanced.causation: The question of whether the Depression was preventable — and by what policy choices — has been one of the central questions in economic history. Friedman and Schwartz's counterfactual (that competent Federal Reserve action could have prevented the banking crisis and the money supply contraction) has been debated by scholars including Elmus Wicker and Allan Meltzer. The consensus is that better monetary policy could have reduced the Depression's severity but that the gold standard's constraints would have limited recovery regardless.
advanced.significance: The Depression's long-term effects on American political culture — the generation that experienced it maintained unusually cautious financial behavior for decades, creating the 'balance sheet recession' dynamics that Keynesians predicted — have been analyzed by economists including Ulrike Malmendier and Stefan Nagel, who found that Depression-era cohorts maintained lower equity holdings throughout their lives. The Depression shaped political attitudes as well as economic behavior: the generation that experienced it was substantially more favorable to government intervention than those who did not.
advanced.theRecord: The retrospective construction of Depression-era economic statistics — unemployment rates, output, prices — has been one of the most important methodological challenges in economic history. Christina Romer's work on pre-Depression unemployment rates revealed that earlier comparisons had exaggerated how unusual the Depression was relative to previous downturns. Her reconstruction of output and employment data substantially refined the evidentiary base. The methodological challenge is that official statistical infrastructure was far less developed before the 1930s than after — many of the data series we now take for granted were created partly in response to the Depression's revelation of statistical gaps. This means the Depression itself is better documented than earlier downturns, creating a potential bias in comparative assessments of its severity.

foundation.theStory: The Great Depression matters as the event that destroyed the pre-Keynesian theory of government, created the institutional architecture of the modern welfare state and financial regulation, provided the political conditions for fascism in Europe, and established the framework within which subsequent economic crises have been understood and managed. Understanding it is necessary for understanding the New Deal, World War II, Keynesian economics, and contemporary debates about fiscal and monetary policy.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the causes of the Depression (monetarist versus Keynesian versus international explanations), the adequacy of the New Deal response, the racial compromises that shaped New Deal institutions, and the lessons the Depression offers for managing subsequent economic crises.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Depression matters for understanding the interwar period, the political conditions for fascism, the New Deal, Keynesian economics, the welfare state, financial regulation, and the 2008 financial crisis response.
questions: The Federal Reserve allowed the money supply to contract by a third during the Depression. Friedman and Schwartz argue this turned a recession into a catastrophe. Bernanke cited this analysis in justifying the aggressive 2008 response. Does the Depression prove that monetary policy is the most important lever for economic stability, or does the Keynesian emphasis on fiscal policy capture something monetary policy misses?

Countries that left the gold standard earlier recovered faster from the Depression. This suggests that the commitment to gold — which prevented monetary expansion — was a major cause of the Depression's depth. What does this reveal about the relationship between international monetary arrangements and domestic economic policy?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential texts are Friedman and Schwartz's A Monetary History of the United States for the monetarist account, Eichengreen's Golden Fetters for the international account, Katznelson's Fear Itself for the political history of the New Deal, and Keynes's General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money (1936) for the theoretical response. Bernanke's Essays on the Great Depression (2000) collects his academic work before becoming Fed Chairman.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The Depression is one of the most studied economic events in history, and the data — unemployment, production, prices, money supply — have been extensively reconstructed and analyzed. The main interpretive challenges are separating causes (what caused the initial downturn?) from amplifiers (what made it so deep and long-lasting?) and assessing the counterfactual (what would have happened with different policies?).
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about the Depression must engage with the monetarist-Keynesian debate by taking a position on the relative importance of monetary and fiscal factors in causing and ending the Depression. It must address the gold standard's role — whether it was the binding constraint that prevented recovery — with the comparative evidence from countries that left gold at different times. And it must engage with the New Deal's racial compromises.
questions: Roosevelt cut spending in 1937 in response to deficit concerns, triggering a sharp recession within the Depression. This is widely cited as evidence for the Keynesian view that premature fiscal consolidation is self-defeating. Does this episode constitute a natural experiment that settles the debate, or are there enough confounding factors to leave the question open?

The Depression produced both the New Deal in America and fascism in Germany — demonstrating that economic catastrophe can generate very different political responses depending on institutional context. What determined which response prevailed in which country, and what does this tell us about the relationship between economic crisis and political outcomes?

research.topic: The role of the gold standard in transmitting and deepening the Depression
research.content: Eichengreen's Golden Fetters (1992) and his subsequent work with Jeffrey Sachs established one of the most robust findings in economic history: countries that left the gold standard earlier recovered faster from the Depression. The gold standard prevented monetary expansion by tying money supply to gold reserves; countries that broke the link could expand money supply and credit, stimulating recovery. This finding has been replicated across multiple datasets and countries and represents the closest thing to a consensus explanation for the Depression's international dimension.

research.topic: The long-term effects of Depression exposure on individual behavior and political attitudes
research.content: Recent research using birth cohort data has found that individuals who experienced the Depression during formative years maintained systematically different economic behaviors and political attitudes throughout their lives — lower equity holdings, higher savings rates, greater support for government intervention. Malmendier and Nagel's work on Depression-era cohorts' financial behavior is the most influential finding. Research on the political attitudes of Depression survivors has found persistently higher support for redistribution and government intervention. The mechanisms (direct experience versus cultural transmission) are debated.

research.topic: The counterfactual trajectory without Federal Reserve failure
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: a controlled experiment in which the Federal Reserve responded differently to the 1930–1933 banking crisis — preventing bank failures and maintaining the money supply — while all other conditions remained the same, allowing direct comparison with the actual Depression trajectory. Why unavailable: the counterfactual cannot be tested; it can only be modeled, and models of the Depression economy are contested. What can be established: the correlation between monetary contraction and economic decline, and the comparative evidence from countries with different monetary responses. What cannot be established: the precise magnitude of the Depression that better monetary policy would have produced, or whether fiscal stimulus would also have been necessary for full recovery.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Policy Failure
comparativeNarrative.content: Economists in the monetarist tradition, led by Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz, argue that the Depression was above all a failure of the Federal Reserve. The Fed stood by as thousands of banks collapsed between 1930 and 1933, allowing the money supply to shrink by roughly a third. On this reading, the 1929 crash did not have to produce a decade of misery. Better decisions by the central bank could have kept the banking system intact and limited the downturn to a fairly ordinary recession. The lesson drawn is that central banks must lend freely in a crisis and stop the money supply from collapsing. That lesson shaped how the Fed acted in 2008, when Ben Bernanke — a Depression scholar — moved aggressively to prevent a repeat of the 1930s bank failures.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Demand Collapse
comparativeNarrative.content: The Keynesian reading starts from a different problem. In the early 1930s, households and businesses wanted to save more than they wanted to spend or invest. That gap produced a downward spiral: less spending meant fewer sales, fewer sales meant more layoffs, more layoffs meant even less spending. John Maynard Keynes argued that no amount of interest-rate cutting could fix this on its own, because even cheap money cannot induce spending when people are too frightened to borrow. Only government spending could fill the hole. On this reading, the New Deal pointed in the right direction but did not go far enough — Roosevelt spent too little and too inconsistently. His decision to cut spending in 1937, worried about the deficit, pushed the economy back into recession and confirmed that withdrawing government support too soon was self-defeating.

comparativeNarrative.name: The System's Crisis
comparativeNarrative.content: A third reading places the blame not on bad decisions by officials but on how the market system itself operates. The speculation of the 1920s, on this view, was not an accident or an aberration. It was the natural result of an economy with no serious rules on finance and no floor under wages or incomes. Left to itself, capitalism tends to generate booms followed by busts that can destroy the political conditions democracy needs. The New Deal, on this reading, was not just a fix for a broken economy. It was a transformation of the system — the creation of deposit insurance, labor protections, and welfare programs that gave capitalism the political legitimacy it could not sustain on its own. The Depression's real lesson was that markets need institutions around them, not just better management of the money supply.

rabbitHole.label: The Interwar Period
rabbitHole.reason: The Great Depression was the central event of the interwar period — its economic devastation created the political conditions for fascism in Europe and reshaped the political landscape of every democratic country.

rabbitHole.label: The Weimar Republic
rabbitHole.reason: The Depression destroyed the Weimar Republic's political legitimacy — mass unemployment and economic desperation created the political space in which the Nazi seizure of power became possible.

rabbitHole.label: World War II
rabbitHole.reason: World War II ended the Depression — military spending produced the fiscal stimulus that peacetime governments had been unable to sustain, and wartime production absorbed the unemployed.

rabbitHole.label: Inequality
rabbitHole.reason: The Depression dramatically increased inequality through mass unemployment and asset deflation, and the New Deal response substantially reduced inequality through its tax, labor, and welfare state policies — making the Depression-to-New Deal sequence one of the most important episodes in the history of economic inequality.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The Depression's collapse of international trade and its political consequences — including the rise of fascist nationalism in Germany and Italy — illustrate the relationship between economic crisis and political nationalism.

rabbitHole.label: John Maynard Keynes
rabbitHole.reason: Keynes's General Theory (1936) was the direct theoretical response to the Depression — his argument that governments must use fiscal policy to stabilize aggregate demand became the foundation of postwar economic policy and transformed how governments understood their economic responsibilities.

rabbitHole.label: Banking
rabbitHole.reason: The Great Depression was primarily transmitted and amplified by banking collapse — the failure of thousands of American banks between 1930 and 1933 contracted the money supply by roughly a third — and the entire institutional architecture of modern banking regulation, from deposit insurance to central bank lender-of-last-resort functions, was designed in its wake.

rabbitHole.label: Credit
rabbitHole.reason: The Great Depression was, at its core, a credit collapse: banks stopped extending credit, firms could not finance inventory or payroll, and the contraction of credit turned a financial crisis into a decade-long economic catastrophe. The Depression is the clearest modern demonstration of the entry's claim that credit expansion and contraction are the mechanism through which financial shocks become economic crises.

rabbitHole.label: The New Deal
rabbitHole.reason: The New Deal was the federal response to the Great Depression.

reference.author: Friedman, Milton and Schwartz, Anna
reference.title: A Monetary History of the United States, 1867–1960
reference.annotation: The foundational monetarist account — Friedman and Schwartz's argument that Federal Reserve failure caused the Depression by allowing the money supply to contract is the most influential explanation in the literature.

reference.author: Eichengreen, Barry
reference.title: Golden Fetters: The Gold Standard and the Great Depression, 1919–1939
reference.annotation: The essential international account — Eichengreen's demonstration that countries which left the gold standard earlier recovered faster is one of the most robust findings in economic history.

reference.author: Katznelson, Ira
reference.title: Fear Itself: The New Deal and the Origins of Our Time
reference.annotation: The essential political history — Katznelson's account of how the Southern Democratic bloc shaped the New Deal's racial compromises is necessary for understanding what the New Deal actually did and did not do.

reference.author: Kindleberger, Charles P.
reference.title: The World in Depression, 1929-1939
reference.annotation: The essential account of the Depression's international dimension — Kindleberger argues that the absence of a hegemonic stabilizer turned a financial crisis into a global catastrophe. Essential for understanding why the Depression was international rather than national in character.

reference.author: Keynes, John Maynard
reference.title: The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money
reference.annotation: The foundational theoretical response to the Depression — Keynes's argument that government spending must fill the gap when private demand collapses is the basis of the entry's Keynesian/demand-collapse account and its 1937 lesson.

reference.author: Bernanke, Ben S.
reference.title: Essays on the Great Depression
reference.annotation: The source for the entry's credit-channel argument — Bernanke's thesis that bank failures destroyed lending relationships beyond the money-supply contraction, the interpretation the entry says shaped his 2008 Fed response.

reference.author: Tooze, Adam
reference.title: The Wages of Destruction: The Making and Breaking of the Nazi Economy
reference.annotation: Underpins the significance section's claim that the specific shape of Nazi recovery — built on rearmament and self-sufficiency — set a path to war that the Depression's origins alone did not determine.

reference.author: Romer, Christina D.
reference.title: Spurious Volatility in Historical Unemployment Data
reference.annotation: The basis for the entry's claim that Romer's reconstruction of pre-Depression unemployment data revised understanding of the Depression's magnitude relative to earlier downturns.

reference.author: James, Harold
reference.title: The End of Globalization: Lessons from the Great Depression
reference.annotation: Cited by name and title in the advanced layer for the thesis that the interwar period was a globalization that failed and whose collapse fed the political catastrophes of the 1930s.

reference.author: Eichengreen, Barry and Sachs, Jeffrey
reference.title: Exchange Rates and Economic Recovery in the 1930s
reference.annotation: The specific co-authored study behind the entry's 'most robust result' claim that countries leaving the gold standard earlier recovered faster — distinct from Eichengreen's later monograph already cited.

reference.author: Malmendier, Ulrike and Nagel, Stefan
reference.title: Depression Babies: Do Macroeconomic Experiences Affect Risk Taking?
reference.annotation: The empirical source for the entry's claim that Depression-era cohorts maintained lower equity holdings throughout their lives, illustrating the 'balance sheet recession' legacy.

reference.author: Terkel, Studs
reference.title: Hard Times: An Oral History of the Great Depression
reference.annotation: The collected oral histories the entry invokes as its direct testimony of lived Depression experience, the documentary record behind the general layer's argument about insecurity and humiliation.

commerce.title: The Great Crash 1929
commerce.author: John Kenneth Galbraith
commerce.note: Start here for the financial history — Galbraith's account of the stock market collapse is elegant, readable, and a model of economic writing for general audiences; the essential starting point before engaging more technical accounts.

commerce.title: Freedom from Fear: The American People in Depression and War
commerce.author: David Kennedy
commerce.note: The essential narrative history — Kennedy's Pulitzer-winning account of 1929–1945 gives the Depression its full political and social context; comprehensive, readable, and the standard one-volume account for general readers.

commerce.title: Golden Fetters: The Gold Standard and the Great Depression
commerce.author: Barry Eichengreen
commerce.note: The essential economic history — Eichengreen's argument that the gold standard transmitted and prolonged the Depression across countries is the scholarly consensus position; demanding but the foundation of modern understanding of why the Depression was so severe.

commerce.title: The Grapes of Wrath
commerce.author: John Steinbeck
commerce.note: The essential literary account — Steinbeck's novel about Dust Bowl migrants is the most powerful single document of Depression-era human experience; read alongside the history to understand what the statistics meant for actual families.

commerce.title: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men
commerce.author: James Agee and Walker Evans
commerce.note: The essential documentary account — Agee's prose and Evans's photographs of Alabama sharecroppers during the Depression is one of the great works of American documentary literature; essential for understanding how the Depression was experienced in the rural South.

commerce.title: The Forgotten Man: A New History of the Great Depression
commerce.author: Amity Shlaes
commerce.note: The essential conservative revisionist account — Shlaes argues that New Deal intervention prolonged the Depression by creating uncertainty that discouraged private investment; explicitly written from a free-market perspective that most economic historians find unpersuasive on the evidence. Read as the strongest available version of the anti-Keynesian position — engaging it seriously is more useful than dismissing it, but read it knowing its ideological commitments upfront.

popularCulture.title: The Grapes of Wrath — John Steinbeck (1939)
popularCulture.description: The Joad family leaves Dust Bowl Oklahoma and drives west to California, where they meet hunger, cheating bosses, and open hostility. Steinbeck rode with migrant workers before writing it, so the daily grind of being poor and unwanted feels real. His Joads, though, are gentler and more noble than most families were. And the larger forces driving the crisis stay offstage, reduced to a vague, faceless enemy.

popularCulture.title: Dorothea Lange and the FSA photography project
popularCulture.description: The government paid Dorothea Lange, Walker Evans, and others to photograph the Depression. Lange's Migrant Mother became the era's defining image. These photos were made to win support for New Deal programs, not to record everything evenly. Photographers chose the most striking faces and moments, and sometimes arranged their subjects. The suffering is real, but the camera was pointed where it would do the most political good.

popularCulture.title: Cinderella Man — Ron Howard (2005)
popularCulture.description: Boxer James J. Braddock loses his money in the Depression, then fights his way to the heavyweight title. The film shows the cold apartment and the fear of an empty plate. But the Depression is just scenery for one man's comeback story. Why the crisis happened, and what government did or failed to do, barely come up. Most Depression movies shrink the disaster down to a single hero this way.

popularCulture.title: Hoovervilles and the politics of blame
popularCulture.description: Americans named the shack camps in their cities 'Hoovervilles,' pinning the misery on President Herbert Hoover, who many felt refused to help. The label was a brutal piece of political branding, tying a whole decade of pain to one man's name. The real story is messier than the nickname. Hoover did try some things and held back on others, but those details faded. The insult outlived the facts.

popularCulture.title: Deposit insurance and the architecture of financial safety
popularCulture.description: Congress created the FDIC in 1933 to insure bank deposits. Before it, a failed bank meant your savings simply vanished. Afterward, the government promised to pay you back up to a set limit, and bank runs grew rare. Most Americans now have never seen one. The FDIC may be the Depression's most useful legacy, yet almost nobody thinks about it. When a policy works, it goes quiet, and people forget it is there.

comparativeSummary: Everyone agrees the 1929 crash did not have to become a decade of ruin. The argument is over what turned a bad year into a catastrophe, and the answer you give decides what you would do differently. Monetarists blame the Federal Reserve for letting banks and money vanish. Keynesians blame a collapse in spending that only government could replace. A third tradition blames the market system itself, arguing that finance without rules always breeds booms and busts. Each diagnosis points to a different cure, and each still shapes how leaders fight the crises that came after.
